message.content: Process Injection: ListPlanting involves adversaries exploiting list-view controls to inject malicious code into legitimate processes, thereby evading process-based defenses and potentially elevating privileges. This technique allows arbitrary code execution within the address space of a separate live process, often bypassing security detections. List-view controls, which display collections of items, store settings in a SysListView32 control within the process memory. Adversaries can hijack these controls by copying code into the target process's memory space, using methods such as obtaining a handle to the SysListView32 child via Windows API calls (e.g., FindWindow, EnumWindows) or other injection techniques. To avoid detection, adversaries may use window messages (e.g., PostMessage, SendMessage) to copy payloads incrementally, bypassing the monitored WriteProcessMemory function. The payload is then executed by sending an LVM_SORTITEMS message, triggering the malicious code as a callback. The impact includes stealthy code execution and potential privilege escalation. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual API calls, securing process memory, and employing behavior-based detection systems to identify abnormal process interactions.
